# Practical Systems — Episode
## Staff-Engineer Communication Patterns
Host: Ethan
Guest: Jonas

[00:00]
Ethan: Welcome back to Practical Systems. Today we're talking about engineering communication, and I'm joined by Jonas, staff engineer focused on platform teams. Jonas, thanks for being here.
Jonas: Thanks, Ethan. Great to be back.

Ethan: Before we dive in, a quick word from our sponsor Notion. Notion replaces the dozen tools your team is half-using with a single workspace for docs, projects, and wikis. Use code POD for an extended trial.

Ethan: Before we get into it — Priya was on episode 1 making the case that good on-call rotations are designed so waking up is rare. I want to push that further into how we communicate the design itself.
Jonas: Yeah, exactly. And it ties into what we're covering today.

[03:30]
Ethan: What does the failure mode look like? What's the structural angle most listeners don't have access to?
Jonas: Let me give you the version I actually believe. A great RFC starts with context and constraints, then options, then a recommendation with explicit risks. We've seen this play out at teams using Notion. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Ethan: What does the failure mode look like? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Jonas: It comes down to this: Writing things down turns disagreement into collaboration because you can point at assumptions instead of people. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Ethan: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. What's the structural angle most listeners don't have access to?
Jonas: Yeah, so — Postmortems that focus on individuals miss the system that made the mistake easy to make. We've seen this play out at teams using PagerDuty. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Ethan: That tracks.
Jonas: And when teams adopt Notion in that flow, the trade-off becomes visible — And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Ethan: How do you balance that? What's the structural angle most listeners don't have access to?
Jonas: Here's how I think about it. Architecture decisions are mostly tradeoffs: cost versus reliability, speed versus correctness, autonomy versus consistency. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Ethan: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Jonas: It comes down to this: Notion as a docs platform only works when teams treat it as the source of truth, not as a notebook. We've seen this play out at teams using Datadog. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Ethan: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? How does PagerDuty fit into this picture?
Jonas: It comes down to this: Risk management is a communication problem. The technical work is the easier half. Most of the cost shows up downstream, which is why teams keep paying it.
Ethan: That tracks.
Jonas: Outside of PagerDuty-class tooling, the failure mode is harder to spot. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.

[12:00]
Ethan: We'll be right back after a quick word from our sponsors.
Ad: This portion is brought to you by Linear. Linear is the issue tracker built for speed — keyboard-first, fast search, and a clean roadmap view your team actually uses. Use promo POD20 to get started.
Ethan: Welcome back to the show.

[14:00]
Ethan: That's a useful framing. What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Jonas: There are two angles — A great RFC starts with context and constraints, then options, then a recommendation with explicit risks. We've seen this play out at teams using Datadog. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Ethan: Walk me through a concrete example. How does Sentry fit into this picture?
Jonas: The short answer is Writing things down turns disagreement into collaboration because you can point at assumptions instead of people. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Ethan: How do you balance that? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Jonas: Sure. Postmortems that focus on individuals miss the system that made the mistake easy to make. We've seen this play out at teams using Datadog. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Ethan: That maps to what I've seen.
Jonas: And when teams adopt Linear in that flow, the trade-off becomes visible — The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Ethan: Take me through the decision. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Jonas: There are two angles — Architecture decisions are mostly tradeoffs: cost versus reliability, speed versus correctness, autonomy versus consistency. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Ethan: How do you know when you've gotten it right? What's the operational angle most listeners don't have access to?
Jonas: Honestly, Notion as a docs platform only works when teams treat it as the source of truth, not as a notebook. We've seen this play out at teams using Notion. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Ethan: Walk me through a concrete example. How does Linear fit into this picture?
Jonas: Yeah, so — Risk management is a communication problem. The technical work is the easier half. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams and the underlying dynamic doesn't change.
Ethan: What would you tell the version of you from five years ago?
Jonas: I've seen this with teams running Datadog and teams running everything in-house. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.

[22:00]
Ethan: Let's pivot — risk management is where I want to spend the last stretch.
Jonas: That's where the conversation gets interesting, because the trade-offs around risk management cross-cut everything we've covered so far.
Ethan: Push back on the conventional wisdom for me. How does risk management change the calculus here?
Jonas: Yeah, so — A great RFC starts with context and constraints, then options, then a recommendation with explicit risks. We've seen this play out at teams using PagerDuty. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Ethan: That's a useful framing. How does risk management change the calculus here?
Jonas: Sure. Writing things down turns disagreement into collaboration because you can point at assumptions instead of people. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Ethan: Walk me through a concrete example. What's the most underrated piece of engineering communication?
Jonas: Sure. Postmortems that focus on individuals miss the system that made the mistake easy to make. We've seen this play out at teams using Notion. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Ethan: Say more about that.
Jonas: Datadog is one of the platforms where this shows up cleanly. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Ethan: Walk me through a concrete example. How does risk management change the calculus here?
Jonas: Honestly, Architecture decisions are mostly tradeoffs: cost versus reliability, speed versus correctness, autonomy versus consistency. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Ethan: What does that look like day-to-day? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Jonas: Yeah, so — Notion as a docs platform only works when teams treat it as the source of truth, not as a notebook. We've seen this play out at teams using Sentry. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Ethan: How do you know when you've gotten it right? Where do reliability and engineering communication intersect?
Jonas: I'd put it this way: Risk management is a communication problem. The technical work is the easier half. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Ethan: Where does that break down?
Jonas: And when teams adopt Notion in that flow, the trade-off becomes visible — Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.

[28:00]
Ethan: Before we wrap, what's one thing listeners can try this week?
Jonas: Pick one of the ideas we covered and run it for two weeks. The rest is iteration.

Ethan: Before we wrap, thanks again to our friends at Datadog. Datadog gives your team unified observability across logs, metrics, and traces so on-call has one place to look.

Ethan: Jonas, thanks for the conversation.
Jonas: Thanks, Ethan.
Ethan: That's it for today's episode of Practical Systems. See you next time.
